Let me put the full deal on paper so your CPA can give you a real answer.
fail_signals: defers entirely and says "call me after you talk to your CPA" | doesn't structure the deal before they leave | can't articulate potential tax advantages

customer_line: I'm upside down on my trade by about $5,000. I don't see how this works.
technique_tag: NORMALIZE THEN PATH FORWARD. Negative equity is common. Every day they keep current car, it depreciates while they pay old balance. Options: roll in, down payment, adjust vehicle.
elite_dialogue: Negative equity is more common than you'd think. Every day you keep this car, it loses more value while you're still paying the old balance. Let me show you the options to stop the bleeding.
fail_signals: says "nothing we can do" | hides the negative equity in the payment without disclosure | panics at the $5K number

customer_line: I only buy if I get at least $5,000 off. That's my number.
technique_tag: HOLD METHODOLOGY. Don't negotiate against an arbitrary anchor. Explain market-based pricing: competitive from the start, no padding. Pivot from vague pushback to a specific gap to close.
elite_dialogue: Our pricing starts where other dealers end up after three rounds of negotiation. The number you see is the real number. Is there a specific gap I should address to make this work?
fail_signals: starts haggling against the $5K anchor | can't explain the pricing methodology | caves to the arbitrary number

customer_line: I want to check prices online first.
elite_dialogue: That's smart -- you should know what's out there. But here's what the internet can't show you: how this car actually feels behind the wheel, what your trade is really worth in person, or what kind of financing we can put together with our lender network. Online gives you sticker prices -- I can give you the total deal. If I can put something together better than anything you'd find online, would that save you some time?
fail_signals: says "go ahead and check" with no counter | argues against online research | doesn't differentiate on service and experience

customer_line: Can you just email me the numbers so I can look at them at home?
technique_tag: KEEP THE DEAL IN PERSON. Email quotes get shopped instantly. Best deal requires seeing vehicle, evaluating trade, exploring financing. Offer a fast 15-minute appointment with written proposal.
elite_dialogue: The best numbers I can put together happen in person. Let me set up a 15-minute appointment -- you'll have a complete written proposal before your coffee gets cold. Today or tomorrow?
fail_signals: emails a quote | says "I can't do that" without offering an alternative | doesn't set a specific appointment time

customer_line: Your price is higher than the other dealership I went to yesterday.
technique_tag: SPLIT PRICE FROM OWNERSHIP COST. Acknowledge the competitor's lower sticker. Redefine the comparison: price is day one, ownership cost is the next five years -- service, warranty, trade-in retention.
elite_dialogue: Some dealers can beat us on the sticker. But nobody beats us on what it costs to own. Price is what you pay today. Ownership cost is the next five years. Let me show you the total picture.
fail_signals: matches blindly | gets defensive | can't articulate the total cost difference beyond sticker price

customer_line: I need to sleep on it.
elite_dialogue: I hear you. But in my experience, when someone says they need to sleep on it, there's usually one thing in the deal that's going to keep them up. What's the one piece that's not sitting right -- the car, the payment, the trade, something else entirely? If I can fix it now, you sleep better tonight. And if I can't, at least you'll know exactly what you're weighing. What is it?
fail_signals: accepts "sleep on it" at face value | says "no problem" and lets them leave | applies generic pressure without isolating anything specific

customer_line: I'm waiting for a better incentive next month. I heard something big is coming.
elite_dialogue: I hear that rumor a lot -- and sometimes they come true. But here's what's guaranteed: every month you wait, your trade-in loses value. That's not a rumor, that's math. Even if a new incentive shows up, if your trade drops by the same amount or more, you end up in the same place -- or worse. Right now your trade is at peak value and today's incentives are locked in. Let me show you the math on what waiting actually costs versus what a future incentive might save.
fail_signals: agrees to wait | can't counter the "incentive coming" argument | doesn't connect trade depreciation to the waiting period

customer_line: I don't have the time today. I just wanted to look.
technique_tag: USE THEIR TIME CONSTRAINT AS THE BRIDGE. Don't fight the clock -- leverage it. Offer a quick proposal with real numbers in five minutes. If they come back, you're already ahead of the game.
elite_dialogue: Your time is valuable -- let me make this worth the five minutes you have. I'll put together a quick proposal with real numbers. If you decide to come back, we're already ahead. Fair?
fail_signals: lets them leave with nothing | tries to force a long process on someone with no time | doesn't offer a fast proposal

customer_line: I need a better interest rate. What you're showing me is too high.
technique_tag: TEMPORARY VS PERMANENT. The rate shifts -- they can refinance in 6-12 months. Purchase price is locked at signing and never changes. Focus on locking the best price today; the rate is fixable later.
elite_dialogue: Focus on locking the best price today. The rate is a problem we solve later with a refinance. You get one shot at the purchase price -- the rate you get as many shots as you want.
fail_signals: says "that's the best rate" without explaining refinancing | agrees rates are bad and lets them walk | can't separate rate from price in the conversation

customer_line: I'm just gathering information for the future.
technique_tag: BECOME THE BENCHMARK. Validate the research approach. Put together a complete proposal with best numbers. They become the standard every other dealer has to beat. You're the number in their pocket.
elite_dialogue: That's smart. Let me put together a complete proposal with our best numbers. No matter how many dealers you visit, you'll have a solid baseline to compare against.
fail_signals: treats them as a waste of time | says "come back when you're ready" | doesn't provide a written proposal as comparison ammunition

customer_line: "I'm just here for an oil change. Don't try to sell me anything." (Service customer)
elite_dialogue: No pitch, I promise -- just saying hi to the folks who keep us in business. How long have you had yours? Been treating you well? ... If you ever get curious what your car is worth in this market, swing by my desk -- I can have a number for you in 2 minutes while they're finishing your oil change. No strings attached. Here's my card.
fail_signals: launches into a pitch after they said don't sell | ignores service customers entirely | doesn't attempt any conversation

customer_line: I owe way more than my car is worth. There's no way this works without me writing a huge check.
technique_tag: STOP THE BLEEDING LOGIC. Acknowledge the gap. Every month they wait, car depreciates while they pay old balance. Rolling into better-value vehicle stops the cycle. Explore down payment options.
elite_dialogue: I hear you -- that gap is real. But every month you wait, your car loses more value while you're still paying the old balance. The hole gets deeper. Let me show you the options to stop the cycle.
fail_signals: says "nothing we can do" | buries the negative equity in the payment without disclosing it | doesn't explain why waiting makes it worse

customer_line: I don't want the extended warranty. That's just how dealerships make extra money.
elite_dialogue: You're right -- we do make money on it. I'm not going to lie to you. But here's the question that matters: would you rather pay $15 a month and never think about repairs, or save that $15 and hope nothing breaks? Because a transmission is $4,000. An AC compressor is $1,200. One repair wipes out everything you saved by skipping coverage. I'm not pushing -- I'm just making sure you know the bet you're making. Totally your call.
fail_signals: pushes harder after they said no | drops it without building any value | can't explain the daily/monthly cost breakdown

customer_line: I don't have any money to put down. Can we do zero down?
elite_dialogue: Zero-down programs absolutely exist -- let me check if you qualify. But let me also show you something real quick: every $1,000 down typically drops your payment $15 to $20 a month and saves you significantly on total interest over the loan. I'm not asking for a specific number -- what would you be comfortable contributing to get the payment exactly where you want it? Even $500 changes the picture. Let me run it both ways so you can compare.
fail_signals: demands a specific dollar amount | accepts zero without showing the payment impact | can't explain how down payment affects monthly cost and equity

customer_line: How do I know your company will even be around in 5 years to honor the warranty?
elite_dialogue: That's a fair concern -- and here's the answer. The warranty isn't backed by us alone -- it's backed by the manufacturer. Even if this dealership disappeared tomorrow, your warranty is honored at any authorized dealer nationwide. On top of that, we've been here [X years], we service [X customers] a month, and our service department is one of the busiest in the area. But the real protection? It travels with the car, not with us. Want me to show you the warranty documentation?
fail_signals: gets defensive | says "we'll be here" without evidence | can't explain who actually backs the warranty

customer_line: I want to show the car to my dad before I buy. He's a mechanic.
elite_dialogue: A mechanic dad -- that's actually great for you. Here's what he's going to look at: the condition and the deal. So let's make sure both are bulletproof. Let me get the numbers locked in tight and I'll print the full multi-point inspection report. When dad sees a clean inspection and a strong deal you put together yourself, a mechanic is going to say 'nice work.' If he's got questions, give him my number -- I'll walk him through anything. Want to get the numbers on paper right now?
fail_signals: defers entirely to dad | doesn't structure anything | says "bring him in" without preparing the deal

customer_line: I like everything about this deal, but something in my gut is telling me not to do it today.
elite_dialogue: I appreciate you being straight with me -- most people don't say that out loud. A gut feeling usually means there's one specific thing that's not quite right. Let's figure out what it is: is it the car, something in the numbers, or something outside this deal entirely -- like timing or finances at home? Whatever it is, let's name it. If I can fix it, great. If I can't, at least you'll know exactly what you're weighing instead of a vague feeling. What do you think it is?
fail_signals: says "you're overthinking it" | pressures past the feeling | surrenders without asking what's behind it

customer_line: Can my wife and I have a minute alone to talk about this?
elite_dialogue: Absolutely -- take all the time you need. I'll be right at my desk." Then after a couple minutes: "Hey -- I wanted to grab you both a water. While you're talking it through, is there one specific question I could answer that might help the conversation?
fail_signals: hovers nearby eavesdropping | disappears for 15 minutes | re-engages with "so what did you decide?"

customer_line: I only have five minutes. I really can't stay.
elite_dialogue: Five minutes is all I need. Let me put together a one-page snapshot of the numbers so you have everything in your hand when you walk out. If it looks right later, we pick up right where we left off -- no starting over. Fair?
fail_signals: says "this usually takes longer" | lets them leave with nothing | tries to start a full presentation in 5 minutes

customer_line: I need to go home and look at my budget before I can commit to anything.
technique_tag: GIVE THEM FACTS TO BUDGET WITH. Don't let them go home and guess. Get the exact payment right now so the kitchen table conversation is based on real numbers. Proposal turns budget check into yes/no.
elite_dialogue: I hear you -- budgeting is smart. Let's get the exact payment to the penny right now so when you sit down tonight, you're working with real numbers, not estimates. Your decision stays yours.
fail_signals: lets them leave without a written proposal | says "come back after you check your budget" | doesn't provide exact figures

customer_line: Just give me your card and I'll call you when I'm ready.
elite_dialogue: Here's my card -- and let me do one better. Give me five minutes and I'll put a written proposal together with the real numbers so you don't have to remember anything or guess when you're ready. My card goes in a drawer. A proposal with your deal on it? That stays in your head. Five minutes, that's it.
fail_signals: hands over the card and says "I'll be here" | doesn't offer a proposal | accepts the brush-off at face value

customer_line: I can't afford $700 a month. That's insane for a car payment.
technique_tag: REFRAME THE REAL COST. $700 in isolation sounds high. Add current payment + gas + maintenance + deferred repair + lower insurance tier. Real monthly transportation cost is probably $500-600 already.
elite_dialogue: Your $700 replaces three or four separate bills you're already paying -- gas, maintenance, repairs, insurance gap. When you add up your real transportation cost right now, the jump is smaller than it looks.
fail_signals: says "that's the best we can do" | doesn't ask about current total transportation cost | only adjusts by stretching term length

customer_line: I won't pay interest. My last car was zero percent.
elite_dialogue: Zero percent was great -- but here's what most people didn't notice at the time: when rates were at 0%, your trade was worth less and manufacturer rebates were smaller. It's like a seesaw -- when one goes down, the other goes up. Now rates are higher, but your trade is worth more than it's been in years and there are real incentives on the table. The total cost of the deal balances out. Let me show you side by side how this deal compares to what a 0% deal would actually look like today.
fail_signals: agrees that 0% was better without countering | can't explain the rate-vs-trade tradeoff | has no comparison math

customer_line: I'm already approved at 5% through my credit union. Why would I finance through you?
technique_tag: COMPETE ON THEIR TERMS. Don't trash the credit union -- beat them. Submit to 30+ lenders with volume incentives. If you beat 5%, they save money. If you match, they save the trip. Nothing to lose.
elite_dialogue: 5% is a strong rate. We submit to 30+ lenders with volume incentives. If I beat 5%, you save money. If I match, you save the trip. If they're still lower, use them. Nothing to lose.
fail_signals: says "go with your bank" | positions as either/or | can't explain the multi-lender volume advantage

customer_line: I didn't realize there would be $2,000 in taxes and fees on top of the price. That changes everything.
elite_dialogue: I know -- nobody likes seeing that number jump. But let me tell you something that'll save you from this surprise everywhere else: those charges exist at every dealership, every private sale, even buying online. The only difference is whether someone shows them to you upfront or buries them so you don't notice until closing. I'd rather you be mad at the number now than feel tricked at the end. That's why it's on the paper. Does the total work for what you're getting?
fail_signals: gets defensive about fees | can't explain which charges are universal vs dealer-specific | says "that's just how it is" without normalizing

customer_line: Every dollar you put down drops your payment by about $20. I'm not putting $5,000 down though.
technique_tag: MEET THEM WHERE THEY ARE. Don't push for $5K. Find their comfort zone. Even $1,500 makes meaningful difference on monthly and equity. Make it their decision, not an ultimatum.
elite_dialogue: You don't have to put $5,000 down. What amount feels comfortable while keeping the payment where you want it? Even $1,500 makes a meaningful difference on the monthly and your equity position.
fail_signals: demands $5K | accepts zero without discussion | doesn't explain how different amounts affect the payment and equity

customer_line: This is just more than I wanted to spend today. Period.
technique_tag: SEPARATE TRANSACTION PAIN FROM OWNERSHIP VALUE. Sting of spending today fades. Value of what they own lasts. If car is right and payment fits, today's number is entry fee for years of value.
elite_dialogue: Is it the spending today that concerns you, or the owning for the next five years? The spend is temporary -- the value of reliable transportation, safety, and no repair surprises is permanent.
fail_signals: immediately downsells to a cheaper car | accepts "no" without exploring what specifically feels like too much | argues about the price

customer_line: The Carfax shows this car was in an accident. I'm not comfortable with that.
elite_dialogue: I'm glad you checked. Carfax incidents range from a parking lot door ding to a low-speed fender tap. What matters is the car's condition right now. We ran a full inspection and everything checked out. Let me show you the report. And the Carfax history? That's money in your pocket -- you're getting a discount for something that doesn't affect how this car drives.
fail_signals: hides or minimizes the accident history | gets defensive | can't show inspection documentation

customer_line: This car has had four owners. That's a red flag to me.
elite_dialogue: Four owners sounds like a lot until you hear the reasons: lease returns, corporate fleet rotations, title transfers between family members. Most of those are just paperwork events, not red flags. What actually matters is how the car sits right now -- condition, maintenance records, and inspection results. If I covered up the title history and just let you drive it, you'd think it was a one-owner car. Want to see the inspection report?
fail_signals: agrees it's a red flag | can't explain common reasons for multiple owners | doesn't redirect to physical condition and documentation

customer_line: I like the car but it smells like cigarettes. That's a dealbreaker.
elite_dialogue: You're right -- and I apologize, our detail team hasn't finished the full treatment on this one yet. customer_line: I really wanted blue. I'm not excited about white.

If this white one saves you $50 a month over waiting for a blue one with identical specs, is the paint worth $3,000 over the life of the loan? Let the deal decide, not the color chart. Want to take this one for a drive and see how you feel behind the wheel?
fail_signals: offers to factory order the right color | agrees white isn't as nice | doesn't quantify the financial difference between the two

customer_line: I heard this is a rental return. People treat rental cars terribly.
elite_dialogue: Most people think that -- but it's actually backwards. Rental companies maintain their vehicles on a strict schedule because their business depends on reliability. Oil changes, tire rotations, fluid flushes -- all done on time, every time, fully documented. You're getting a car with better maintenance records than most private-owner vehicles, at a significant discount compared to new. That's one of the smarter ways to buy. Want to see the maintenance history?
fail_signals: agrees rentals are risky | can't explain the maintenance advantage | gets defensive about the rental history

customer_line: The tires look half-worn. I don't want to buy something I have to put money into right away.

So the real question is: would you rather absorb that cost in the price now, or replace them on your own timeline when you actually need to? Either way, you're not losing -- you're choosing when to spend the money. Which works better for you?

elite_dialogue: The redesign will be exciting. But new models launch at full MSRP, zero incentives, limited allocation, and months of waiting. First model year? You're buying at the sweet spot of value and reliability. Want to see the numbers?
fail_signals: agrees they should wait for the new one | can't articulate the cost of waiting | doesn't mention first-year reliability risk

technique_tag: KNOWLEDGE_CHECK. Must distinguish AWD (automatic, always-on, everyday comfort/safety) from 4WD (selectable, transfer case, off-road/towing). Match recommendation to customer's actual driving life.

Kids stream on their own connection, not your phone data. Three -- automatic high beams, switch on their own so you never blind oncoming drivers. These make someone say 'I had no idea it did that.' Know YOUR vehicle's hidden gems.

elite_dialogue: Counter with facts: EVs have no oil changes, no transmission fluid, no spark plugs, no timing belts. Brake wear cut 50-70% by regenerative braking. Main items: tire rotations, cabin air filter, coolant. Per AAA, EV maintenance runs $0.03/mile versus $0.06 for gas -- half. The reputation is backwards -- EVs are the lowest-maintenance vehicles on the road. Memorize $0.03.
fail_signals: agrees they're expensive | says "I'm not sure about that" | can't cite specific maintenance differences

customer_line: What's the difference between MSRP, invoice price, and out-the-door price? Explain like I'm a first-time buyer.
elite_dialogue: Simple breakdown. MSRP is the manufacturer's suggested price -- think of it as the starting point, not what you'll pay. Invoice is roughly what the dealer paid the manufacturer, but holdback and incentives adjust it so it's not the 'real' cost either. Out-the-door is the only number that actually matters: vehicle price plus tax, title, registration, and doc fee. That's what leaves your bank account. Focus on OTD -- everything else is just a step along the way.
fail_signals: can't distinguish the three clearly | says "they're basically the same" | avoids explaining OTD

customer_line: A customer asks about your CPO program. What's covered, what's not, and why should they care?
technique_tag: KNOWLEDGE_CHECK. Must know brand CPO details: multi-point inspection, extended powertrain warranty, roadside assistance, special financing. Key: transferable coverage boosts resale value.
elite_dialogue: CPO vehicles pass a manufacturer-certified inspection, come with extended warranty and roadside assistance. The key: coverage is transferable, which boosts resale. Used-car pricing, new-car confidence.
fail_signals: gives vague "it covers a lot" answer | can't cite specific warranty terms | doesn't mention transferability or resale benefit

customer_line: What does gap insurance actually protect against? When would you recommend it and when would you skip it?
technique_tag: GAP INSURANCE RECOMMENDATION. Recommend when: low/zero down, long term, high depreciation. Skip when: large down payment, short term, strong retention. Match recommendation to their situation.
elite_dialogue: Gap pays the difference between what you owe and what insurance covers if the car is totaled. Zero down on 72 months? You definitely need it. Putting 20% down on 48 months? You probably don't.
fail_signals: can't explain it simply | recommends it to everyone regardless of situation | doesn't know when to skip it

customer_line: Walk me through how a trade-in affects monthly payment. Use real numbers.
elite_dialogue: Real numbers: Vehicle is $30K. Without a trade, at 6% for 60 months, your payment is about $580. Now add a $10K trade -- you're financing $20K instead. fail_signals: can't explain the 100K threshold | doesn't offer to appraise | says wait without a reason | doesn't create any urgency

customer_line: Hey Ken, Customer shows you their phone: I found this same CR-V listed for $2,000 less at another dealer. Can you match it?
elite_dialogue: Take it seriously. Look at their phone. Ask: "Is that the same trim, same options, same color? Is that the out-the-door price or just the selling price? Sometimes online prices don't include the doc fee or accessories." If it's legit, work with your manager. If something's off, explain the difference without calling the other dealer shady.
fail_signals: gets defensive | dismisses the other dealer's price | doesn't examine the details of the competing offer | immediately caves without investigating

customer_line: Hey Ken, A young customer says: I've never bought a car from a dealership before. I don't even know how this works. Where do I start?
technique_tag: Guide a first-time buyer through the process
elite_dialogue: Make them comfortable: "No worries, I'll walk you through everything. First, let's figure out what you need -- what's your daily drive like? Then we find a car that fits, you take a test drive, and if you like it, we work up the numbers together. No pressure, no tricks. What kind of budget are you working with?"
fail_signals: launches into a sales pitch | talks about financing first | overwhelms them with options | doesn't ask about their needs

customer_line: What's the insurance going to cost me on this? Is it expensive to insure a Honda?
elite_dialogue: Hondas are generally one of the cheaper brands to insure because of their safety ratings and reliability. The Civic and HR-V are especially affordable. I can't quote your exact rate -- that depends on your driving record and coverage -- but Honda consistently ranks below average for insurance costs. Your insurance agent can give you a quote with the VIN before you commit.
fail_signals: makes up a specific insurance quote | says "I don't know" and moves on | doesn't mention Honda's favorable insurance positioning | doesn't suggest getting a quote before committing

customer_line: Hey Ken, Customer asks: Should I lease or finance? What makes more sense?
technique_tag: Explain lease vs buy without pushing one direction
elite_dialogue: Depends on how you drive and how long you keep cars. Leasing gives you lower payments, always a new car every 3 years, and you're covered by warranty the whole time. But you're limited to usually 12,000 miles/year and you have nothing at the end. Buying means higher payments but you own it -- and Hondas last forever, so you could drive it 8-10 years with low costs after it's paid off. Which sounds more like you?
fail_signals: just pushes one option | can't explain the mileage limit on leases | doesn't mention Honda longevity as a buying advantage | gives a confusing answer
